subject: Internal medicine
title: Hyperkalemia
preambleMarkdown:
# Hyperkalemia



> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Hyperkalemia is a clinical condition or syndrome in internal medicine.
- This article focuses on adult presentation, differential diagnosis, diagnostics, complications, chronic management, and prevention.
- The practical clinical priority is internal medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Hyperkalemia to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Hyperkalemia is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is internal medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, or resolved state.
- Mild, moderate, severe, complicated, or life-threatening disease.
- Primary disease versus secondary complication or medication effect.
- Outpatient, emergency department, inpatient, ICU, pregnancy, pediatric, or older-adult setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Hyperkalemia depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In internal medicine, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Hyperkalemia.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Hyperkalemia disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Hyperkalemia should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Hyperkalemia first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Hyperkalemia, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Symptoms and signs depend on acuity, organ system involved, severity, comorbidities, medications, and prior episodes.
- Classic findings help recognition but absence of a classic finding does not exclude serious disease.
- Complications may be the first presentation in older adults, immunocompromised patients, and patients with limited follow-up.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Hyperkalemia, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused internal medicine examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- ECG changes, weakness, severe elevation, kidney failure, acidosis, or rapidly rising potassium require urgent treatment.
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Hyperkalemia presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Hyperkalemia with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Hyperkalemia is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Hyperkalemia should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize myocardium with IV calcium when ECG changes or severe toxicity is present.
- Shift potassium intracellularly with insulin/glucose and beta agonist when indicated; remove potassium with diuretics, binders, or dialysis depending on context.
- Assess stability first, then treat the underlying cause and the complications already present.
- Use supportive care, medications, procedures, rehabilitation, and prevention according to severity and contraindications.
- Escalate to specialty care when diagnosis is uncertain, severe disease is present, or treatment risk is high.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Hyperkalemia is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses internal medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Hyperkalemia, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Hyperkalemia may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Hyperkalemia patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Hyperkalemia, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Hyperkalemia in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Hyperkalemia, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Hyperkalemia does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Hyperkalemia may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Hyperkalemia, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Hyperkalemia as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Calcium stabilizes the cardiac membrane but does not lower potassium.
- Insulin shifts potassium intracellularly.
- Dialysis is definitive in severe refractory hyperkalemia with renal failure.
- Know the classic presentation, first diagnostic step, first treatment step, dangerous complication, and follow-up need.
- Severity and stability decide disposition.
- Treatment should include prevention of recurrence when possible.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Hyperkalemia draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.

### Source Excerpt: Hyperkalemia

_Source lines: 28007-28180._

```text
Hyperkalemia

A. Causes
1. Increased total-body potassium
a. Renal failure (acute or chronic)
b. Hypoaldosterone states: Addison disease, hyporeninemic
hypoaldosteronism, ACE inhibitors, potassium-sparing diuretics
(spironolactone)
c. Iatrogenic-excessive doses of potassium (use caution when
administering potassium in patients with renal failure) and
certain medications (e.g., trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole)
d. Blood transfusion-usually due to lysed cells
Quick HIT
Hyperkalemia inhibits renal ammonia synthesis and reabsorption.

Thus, net acid excretion is impaired and results in metabolic
acidosis. This further exacerbates hyperkalemia due to K+
movement out of cells.

1. Redistribution-translocation of potassium from intracellular to
extracellular space
a. Acidosis (notably not seen in lactic or ketoacidosis)
b. Tissue/cell breakdown-rhabdomyolysis (muscle breakdown),
chemotherapy, hemolysis, burns
c. GI bleeding
d. Insulin deficiency-insulin stimulates the Na + -K + -ATPase and
causes K + to shift into cells. Therefore, insulin deficiency and
hypertonicity (high glucose) promote K + shifts from ICF to ECF
e. Rapid administration of beta-blocker
2. Pseudohyperkalemia (spurious)
a. This refers to an artificially elevated plasma K + concentration
due to K + movement out of cells immediately before or after
venipuncture. Contributing factors include prolonged use of a
tourniquet with or without repeated fist clenching. This can
cause acidosis and subsequent K + loss from cells. Nevertheless,
plasma (not serum) K + should be normal. (Repeat the test to
confirm this.)

b. In addition, if the sample is not processed quickly, some red
blood cells will hemolyze and cause spillage of K + leading to a
falsely elevated result. The test should be repeated in this case.

c. Other contributing factors include leukocytosis and
thrombocytosis.

An older man is admitted to the hospital with weakness and palpitations,
and found to have hyperkalemia. An ECG is performed and shows
peaked T waves.

Which of the following is NOT a cause of this electrolyte abnormality?

A. Excessive insulin administration
B. Metabolic acidosis
C. Ischemic bowel
D. Excessive beta-blocker administration
E. Excessive ACE inhibitor administration
- The answer is A: Excessive insulin administration. The classic
ECG changes in hyperkalemia are peaked T waves, an increased PR
interval, and an increased QRS width. If left untreated, the QRS
complexes will progress to a sine wave pattern. All of the following
will increase serum potassium concentrations: (D, E) anything that
decreases the activity of the sodium-potassium ATPase (e.g., beta-
blockers, digoxin, ACE inhibitors/ARBs and hypoaldosteronism,
insulin deficiency); (B) anything that causes acidemia and a
transcellular shift of hydrogen ions into cells and potassium ions out
of cells; (C) anything causing massive cell death/lysis (e.g., ischemic
bowel, hemolysis, rhabdomyolysis, tumor lysis syndrome); and
anything causing renal disease or a decrease in effective arterial
volume (e.g., CHF, liver failure) that leads to the inability to excrete
potassium. Also, be aware of pseudohyperkalemia, in which the
laboratory sample that was drawn undergoes hemolysis and gives a
falsely high serum potassium value.

B. Clinical Features
1. Arrhythmias-The most important effect of hyperkalemia is on the
heart (Figure 8-5). Check an ECG immediately in a hyperkalemic
patient. With increasing potassium, ECG changes progress through
tall, peaked T waves, QRS widening, PR interval prolongation, loss
of P waves, and finally a sine-wave pattern
2. Muscle weakness and (rarely) flaccid paralysis
3. Decreased deep tendon reflexes
4. Respiratory failure
5. Nausea/vomiting, intestinal colic, diarrhea
6. Acidosis due to decreased ammonium formation in renal tubules
Quick HIT
ECG changes in hyperkalemia become prominent when K+ >6 and
include:
- Peaked T waves (by 10 mm)

- A prolonged PR interval
- Widening of QRS and merging of QRS with T wave
- Ventricular fibrillation and cardiac arrest (with increasing levels
of K+ )
FIGURE
8-5 Diagnostic evaluation of hyperkalemia.

(Adapted with permission from Humes HD, DuPont HL, Gardner LB, et al. Kelley's Textbook
of Internal Medicine. 4th ed. Lippincott Williams & Wilkins; 2000:1168. Figure 147-1.)

Quick HIT
Of all electrolyte disturbances, hyperkalemia is the most dangerous
and can be the most rapidly fatal.

C. Treatment
1. If the hyperkalemia is severe, or if ECG changes are present, first

give IV calcium.

a. Calcium stabilizes the resting membrane potential of the
myocardial membrane-that is, it decreases membrane
excitability.

b. Use caution in giving calcium to patients on digoxin.

(Hypercalcemia predisposes the patient to digoxin toxicity.)

2. Shift potassium into the intracellular compartment.

a. Glucose and insulin-Works via stimulation of Na + /K + pump.

Glucose alone will stimulate insulin from beta-cells, but exogenous
insulin is more rapid (and thus should be one of the first steps
in management). Give both to prevent hypoglycemia.

b. beta-Agonists (e.g., albuterol)-Also works via stimulation of Na + /
K+ pump. Transient management in those with symptoms or
ECG changes.

c. Sodium bicarbonate-Increases pH level, which shifts K + into
cells via H + /K + pump. Similar to beta-agonists, an emergency
measure in severe hyperkalemia.

3. Remove potassium from the body.

a. Diuretics (e.g., furosemide)
b. GI cation exchangers: newer agents patiromer or sodium
zirconium cyclosilicate are now preferred to kayexalate (sodium
polystyrene sulfonate) if available. These help to bind and
remove potassium in the GI tract. Hemodialysis.

- Most rapid and effective way of lowering plasma K + .

- Reserved for intractable hyperkalemia (usually over 7) and
for those with renal failure.

Magnesium
Overview
- Normal Mg2+ levels: 1.8 to 2.5 mg/dL.

- Location in the body-Most of the magnesium in the body (two-
thirds) is in bones. The remainder (one-third) is intracellular. Only 1%
of magnesium is extracellular.

- Influences on magnesium excretion-Many hormones can alter
urinary magnesium excretion (e.g., insulin/glucagons, PTH,
calcitonin, ADH, and steroids).

- Magnesium absorption and balance-About 30% to 40% of dietary
magnesium is absorbed in the GI tract, but this percentage increases
when magnesium levels are low. The kidney has a great capacity to
reabsorb magnesium and is the major regulator of magnesium

balance.
```

### Source Excerpt: Potassium Imbalance

_Source lines: 15088-15296._

```text
II. POTASSIUM IMBALANCE
A. Hypokalemia

## 1. Discussion

a. Definition. Most common electrolyte abnormality, serum potassium level
that is less than 3.5 mEq/L
b. Causes
i. Extrarenal causes. Inadequate dietary intake, diarrhea, vomiting, and
redistribution (e.g., as a result of insulin administration, epinephrine
infusion, or acute alkalemia)
ii. Renal causes
a) Drug-induced renal losses. Loop diuretics, penicillin, aminoglyco-
sides, and amphotericin B
b) Hormone-induced renal losses. Can occur as a result of primary
adrenal adenomas, adrenal hyperplasia, ectopic adrenocorticotropic
hormone syndrome, renin-secreting tumors, renal artery stenosis,
and malignant hypertension
c) Renal tubular acidosis, Bartter syndrome, or chronic magnesium
depletion can also lead to hypokalemia.

2. Clinical features
a. Symptoms. Weakness, paresthesias, and polyuria
b. Physical examination findings. Areflexia, orthostatic hypotension, ileus,
paralysis, and arrhythmias

## 3. Evaluation

a. Laboratory studies should include a serum electrolyte panel; serum BUN,
creatinine, creatinine phosphokinase, phosphate, magnesium, and glucose
levels; and urinalysis.

i. Serum evaluation may show elevated creatinine phosphokinase levels.

ii. Urinalysis. The urine specimen may be dipstick-positive for red
blood cells. Formal urinalysis may reveal myoglobin, consistent with
rhabdomyolysis.

b. Electrocardiography. ECG findings include T-wave flattening or inversion,
U waves (Figure 7-1), ST-segment depression, premature ventricular con-
tractions, and a wide QRS complex.

## 4. Therapy

a. Potassium replacement
i. Serum potassium level is greater than 2.5 mEq/L, no ECG abnormalities;
40 to 80 mEq of potassium chloride should be administered per day
until the imbalance is corrected, with no more than 40 mEq given as a
single dose.

ii. Severe hypokalemia, potassium level less than 2.5 mEq/L, is treated by
infusing 10 mEq of potassium chloride per hour in 50 to 100 mL of
5% dextrose in water or normal saline by intravenous piggyback for 3 to
4 hours.

a) No more than 40 mEq of potassium should ever be put in a single
liter of intravenous fluid, and no more than 10 mEq should be given
per hour.

b) Continuous cardiac monitoring is required.

b. Magnesium replacement with 2 g of magnesium sulfate in 50 mL of
5% dextrose in water administered over 20 minutes may be necessary.

186 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Diseases of the Cardiovascular System
Metabolic Emergencies
c. Phosphate replacement. If the serum phosphate level is low, potassium
phosphate may be used instead of potassium chloride. The recommended
daily dose is 2.5 mg/kg.

## 5. Disposition

a. Admission
i. Patients with serum potassium concentrations of less than 2.5 mEq/L
require admission to the hospital.

ii. Patients with malignant cardiac dysrhythmias, digitalis toxicity, pro-
found weakness with impending respiratory failure, rhabdomyolysis,
hepatic encephalopathy, or a serum potassium level of less than
2.0 mEq/L require admission to the intensive care unit.

b. Discharge. Patients with mild hypokalemia (serum potassium concentration $
2.5 to 3.5 mEq/L) can usually be managed as outpatients with gradual oral
potassium repletion, provided they do not have ECG abnormalities, profound
muscular weakness, ileus, or other serious effects. Patients who are discharged
on oral supplementation should have a follow-up appointment within 48 to
72 hours.

B. Hyperkalemia

## 1. Discussion

a. Definition. Serum potassium level that exceeds 5.5 mEq/L
b. Causes
i. Extrarenal causes. Insulin deficiency, acidemia, hyperosmolality,
' blocker administration, oral or intravenous potassium supplements,
penicillin potassium salts, massive blood transfusion, crush injuries,
burns, mesenteric or muscular infarction, and tumor lysis syndrome.

ii. Renal causes. Chronic renal insufficiency, acute renal failure, hypoal-
dosteronism, and drugs (e.g., nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs,
cyclosporine, heparin, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors,
potassium-sparing diuretics) can also cause hyperkalemia.

iii. Laboratory error is the most common cause of hyperkalemia.

2. Clinical features
a. Symptoms. Include weakness, paresthesias, and confusion
b. Physical examination findings. Paralysis, areflexia, ileus, respiratory insuf-
ficiency, or cardiac arrest
F I G U R E
7-1 	Electrocardiogram with U waves.

M E T A B O L I C E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	187

Diseases of the Cardiovascular System
Metabolic Emergencies
3. Differential diagnoses include pseudohyperkalemia, which can occur as a
result of hemolysis, extreme leukocytosis, acidosis, thrombocytosis, or cold
agglutinins.

## 4. Evaluation

a. Laboratory studies include a serum electrolyte panel, serum BUN, creati-
nine, glucose, and magnesium levels.

b. Electrocardiography. The patient should be placed on a continuous
cardiac monitor, and an ECG should be obtained. Early ECG findings
include peaked T waves and a shortened QT interval. Later, widened
QRS complexes, prolonged PR intervals, low-amplitude P waves, and el-
evation or depression of the ST segment are seen. Advanced changes in-
clude absent P waves, marked QRS complex widening, and tall T waves,
resulting in a sine wave pattern, ventricular fibrillation, and asystole
(Figure 7-2).

## 5. Therapy

a. Acute therapy
i. Calcium chloride or calcium gluconate is administered over 2 minutes
and repeated in 5 to 10 minutes if necessary. By stabilizing cell membranes
Quick 	HIT
If the potassium level is ex-
tremely high or if the sample
was hemolyzed, check
another blood sample.

A
B
F I G U R E
7-2 	(A) ECG findings of severe hyperkalemia. Peaked T waves with
pseudoinfarction pattern. (B) Repeat ECG in the same patient after
hyperkalemia treatment.

(Used by permission of Martin Huecker, MD.)

188 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Diseases of the Cardiovascular System
Metabolic Emergencies
without altering potassium levels, calcium protects the patient against
malignant arrhythmia and is the most important first step in treatment.

Calcium chloride should ideally be infused through a central line as it
causes severe skin injury if accidentally infiltrated from peripheral intrave-
nous lines.

ii. Sodium bicarbonate (44 mEq [one ampule] administered intravenously
over 5 minutes and repeated 10 to 15 minutes later if necessary) causes
an intracellular influx of potassium. Onset of action occurs in approxi-
mately 15 minutes.

iii. Regular insulin (10 to 20 U administered via an intravenous push) with
10% dextrose (500 mL in water) administered over 1 hour, or 10 U of insu-
lin administered via an intravenous push with one or two ampules (25 g) of
50% glucose administered over 5 minutes will lower potassium by causing
an intracellular shift. Effects occur 30 to 60 minutes after administration.

iv. Furosemide, bumetanide , and acetazolamide all increase potassium
excretion.

v. Dialysis should be considered for patients with severe hyperkalemia
who have failed to respond to pharmacologic attempts at lowering the
potassium level and for patients with acute or chronic renal failure.

b. Maintenance of potassium balance. Potassium balance is maintained by:
i. Diuretics and fludrocortisone
ii. Cation-exchange resins, such as sodium polystyrene sulfonate
iii. Aldosterone, either as desoxycorticosterone acetate or fludrocortisone
acetate

## 6. Disposition

a. Admission. When ECG abnormalities or clinical manifestations of hyper-
kalemia are present, admission to the intensive care unit with continuous
cardiac monitoring is required.

b. Discharge. Patients with mild serum potassium elevations in the absence of
clinical and ECG abnormalities can be discharged, provided any identifiable
predisposing factors have been corrected. Patients should have a follow-up
evaluation within 48 to 72 hours.
```
